HE rapid proliferation of satellites, fueled by the com-
c
[  mercialization of space, has significantly amplified the
  T
  threat posed by space debris. In 2023, more than 5,000 active
2
satellites were orbiting the Earth [1], alongside over 40,500
v
trackable objects larger than 10 cm [2], and millions of smaller,
0
7 untrackable fragments. These objects,  traveling at speeds of
9 up to 28,000 km/h, pose severe risks to both satellites and
6 crewed spacecraft. Collisions between debris and active satel-
0
. lites generate additional debris, which can trigger a cascade
1 effect known as the Kessler syndrome. This phenomenon could
0 render certain orbits unusable for decades [3]. Consequently,
5
monitoring space debris has become a critical priority for
2
: both governmental and commercial space organizations. This
v need for surveillance underscores  the importance of Space
i
Domain Awareness (SDA), which is defined as the capability
X
r to detect, track, and predict the movement of objects in space.
a SDA not only includes  tracking in-orbit  satellites but also
relies on ground-based elements necessary for comprehensive
situational awareness [4].
Current  SDA systems heavily depend on ground-based
sensors, which face significant challenges in real-time data
acquisition, accuracy, and coverage, making them vulnerable
to single points of failure and limiting their ability to provide
continuous, global surveillance [5]–[7]. To address these lim-
itations, decentralized on-orbit (or space-based) architectures
are gaining attention as a promising solution, particularly for
integrating optical sensors to enhance debris detection [6], [7].
Additionally,  the integration of blockchain technology is
emerging as an effective  tool  for enhancing data security
in space systems. The decentralized nature of blockchain

rveillance: Blockchain for
nt Debris Tracking
∗  †
es Karabulut Kurt  , and Enver Ozdemir
al Engineering, Polytechnique Montre´al, QC, Canada
hnical University, Istanbul, Tu¨rkiye
logy, TU¨ B˙ITAK B˙ILGEM, Kocaeli, Tu¨rkiye
provides a tamper-resistant record of transactions [8], ensuring
the integrity and authenticity of data shared between satellites.
By integrating blockchain into SDA systems,  satellites can
securely validate and exchange debris tracking data, enabling
trusted,  real-time coordination in response to potential col-
lision threats [9],  [10]. Previous studies have demonstrated
that blockchain can secure satellite communications and data
exchanges, especially in decentralized networks where tradi-
tional centralized control mechanisms are less practical [11].
Blockchain has also been explored as a solution for managing
space traffic,  allowing the detection of anomalous satellite
behavior and improving overall system reliability [12].
As  the number of satellites continues  to grow,  effective
and collaborative management becomes increasingly essential,
particularly given that  these satellites are owned by various
entities across different countries. A centralized authority may
not be suitable for tasks like debris management, safe route
planning, or mitigating surface-based attacks. Instead, a decen-
tralized and transparent communication model  is preferable,
making blockchain technology an ideal solution.
A. Contributions
This paper makes the following contributions:
• We propose a network model  that  integrates with the
current state-of-the-art optical decentralized surveillance
network, enhancing its efficiency and scalability.
• We leverage blockchain technology to ensure secure and
tamper-resistant record-keeping to maintain data integrity
and traceability.
• We develop a role-based consensus mechanism that opti-
mizes network performance by assigning dedicated roles
for verification and approval phases.
B. Structure
The rest of this paper is organized as follows. Section II

case. Section IV presents  the mathematical model. Section
V presents  the numerical  results. Section VI discusses  the
resulting integrated network. Section VII concludes the paper.
II. PRELIMINARY CONCEPTS
Blockchain is a distributed digital database that does not
reside on a single server but is shared between multiple nodes.
Data are stored in blocks on the blockchain, and these blocks

text:
Recent advancements  in optical  space-based surveillance
networks  have  demonstrated  significant  improvements  in

Figure 3: Overview of the proposed network, illust
tracking LEO debris. In [7], a multi-satellite optical tracking
network was proposed, operating with a measurement interval
of 120 seconds, and utilizing multiple point-of-view (MPOV)
observations for more accurate orbit determination. The op-
timal network consists of 24 satellites distributed, ensuring
continuous coverage of the 700–1,000 km altitude range. The
study also found that a 120-second interval provided the lowest
mean errors. Building upon this foundation, we propose a
secure and efficient blockchain space-based network aimed at
enhancing the integrity and reliability of debris tracking data,
as illustrated in Fig. 3.
A. Key Constraints for Validation and Design
We must meet the two following constraints, as expressed
in Eq. 1,  to ensure our network aligns with the design and
operates effectively within the established optimal points of
the optical network:
(i) Satellite Count: The baseline network consists of 24
satellites. If the number of satellites in our network ex-
ceeds this threshold, we must apply network partitioning
strategies to maintain performance.
(ii) Total Delay: The total delay incurred by all operations
performed in our algorithm must be completed within the

rating the roles and functions of nodes (satellites).
120-second measurement window. This ensures that the
blockchain operations do not interfere with the core data
collection process and maintain synchronization with the
optical surveillance network.

B. Roles and Functions
In our paper,  nodes  (formerly referred  to as  satellites)
perform different roles and functions to facilitate consensus
and support the optical surveillance network.
1) Roles: define the participation in the blockchain consen-
sus process. A node has to take at least one of the following
roles:
a) Approver:  responsible for validating blocks and de-
ciding whether a new block can be added to the blockchain.
Only the approvers participate in the approval process of a
block.
b) Verifier: responsible for authenticating network pack-
ets to ensure the integrity and correctness of the data trans-
mitted across the network.
2) Functions: define the tasks a node performs in addition
to its role within the consensus:

The proposed algorithm operates in three key phases, where
all nodes in the network are assumed to have a trust relation-
ship with each other.
A. Key Generation Phase
Let S = {U1, . . . , Un} denote the nodes in the network.
In the key generation phase, described in Algorithm 1,  the
group manager is responsible for setting up the cryptographic
parameters for the network and all its nodes. Specifically:
• Public values: x1, . . . , xn are public values (identifying
information of nodes);
• Private values: f(x1), . . . , f(xn) are private values as-
signed to nodes U1, . . . , Un respectively;
• Elliptic Curve Setup: The finite field Fp, where p is a
prime number and an elliptic curve E over Fp. f(x) is
a degree t − 1 polynomial over Fp and x1, . . . , xn are
elements of a finite field Fp;
• Reference Polynomial: The polynomial f(x) of degree
t − 1.
B. Mining Phase
Once the keys are established, the approver nodes perform
the task of validating and approving blocks. To add a new
block to the blockchain, at least t out of n nodes must partici-
pate in this phase, detailed in Algorithm 2. Each approver gets
the public identifier of the others. Each computes its ck values
and releases ck publicly to be included in the block where it
can be expressed. When nodes achieve this result, the identity
information of the nodes participating in the approval process
is included in the block, and added to the blockchain.

Algorithm 2: Mining Phase
1 At least t out of n available nodes join the mining
process. They are called approvers.
2 Each miner gets the public identity information of
others.
3 Each miner computes:

C. Verifying Phase
After  the mining phase,  the verifier nodes  take over  in
the verifying phase, responsible for ensuring the integrity of
the transmitted network packets. Each verifier node ensures
that the data being transmitted between nodes is accurate and
unaltered. The phase is described in Algorithm 3 and requires
at least v verifier nodes to authenticate the network packets.
The verifiers (at least deg(f(x))+1 verifiers needed) compute
the sum of ck’s and confirm the result is f(0)P . Then they
sign the results by computing dk and get the sum of all and
put the sum in the block.

The experiments were conducted on a Windows 10 machine
with an AMD Ryzen 7 5800HS processor (3.20 GHz) and
16 GB of RAM. The implementation was written in Go and
executed within the Windows Subsystem for Linux (WSL).
The simulation parameters used  in  these experiments are
summarized in Table I.
To evaluate the network’s performance, we use two key
metrics:  throughput  and response  time. We  then  test  our

consensus model, which incorporates varying role distribu-
tions,  against  state-of-the-art  approaches  that  rely on full
participation. Different configurations were tested by adjusting
the percentage of verifiers, approvers, and shared-role nodes.
The role assignment strategies are outlined in Table II.
Table II: Roles distribution for varying total node count.

Throughput  results, depicted in Fig. 4,  serve as  the pri-
mary metric for assessing the network’s capacity to handle
transactions.  It  is calculated by dividing the  total number
of transactions successfully committed by the execution time
measured in seconds. Using the results in [13] as a benchmark,
our configurations achieve a notable throughput of 1531 TX/s
with fewer  than 20 nodes, outperforming PBFT, which is
reported to reach 1000 TX/s. Notably, no benchmark results
are available beyond 20 nodes; however, we have extended
the benchmark up to 50 nodes, as this is still considered a
small-scale network.
Additionally, the results for the selective participation con-
figuration show that throughput increases from 1391 TX/s at
15 nodes to 1931 TX/s at 30 nodes. Beyond this, throughput
declines, dropping to 560 TX/s at 50 nodes. This informs us
that, while the network can efficiently process  transactions
with a moderate number of nodes, scaling beyond this point
leads to diminishing returns due to increased communication
overhead. Comparatively, in the fully participatory consensus
configuration, throughput starts at just 546 TK/s for 15 nodes
and decreases rapidly, reaching only 10 TK/s at 50 nodes, thus
never meets the benchmark in [13].
Response  time  is another  critical metric  for  evaluating
the network’s  efficiency and measures  the  total  delay  in
this process, encompassing both the verification and approval
phases. Results are shown in Fig. 5. As the number of nodes
increases, the response time initially increases for the selective

nodes. The peaks are explained by the higher contention for
roles and the relatively smaller pool of nodes handling both the
verification and approval processes. However, as we increase
nodes,  the response time drops to 5.37 seconds (30 nodes).
In contrast, the configuration of fully participatory consensus
experiences a sharp increase in response time as the number of
nodes increases and then continues to rise, with 58.3 seconds
at 50 nodes.
In the proposed consensus algorithm, not all nodes participate
in the verification and approval processes. Optimal perfor-
mance is achieved with approximately 20 - 30 nodes (indicated
by the symbol ⋄ in Fig. 4 and Fig. 5),  striking a balance

We can then describe the three key phases that underpin the
operation of the network (integrated with [7]) in Fig. 3:
(Phase 1)  Data Generation (LEO Debris Tracking)
Every 120 seconds, satellites collect optical track-
ing data on debris, which includes position, ve-
locity, and uncertainties. This data is crucial for
accurate orbital predictions.  It  is then packaged
as a blockchain transaction to be processed by
the network (  1 → 2  ).
(Phase 2)  Blockchain Processing (Decentralized Valida-
tion)
The 24-node blockchain network processes and
stores  the  tracking data.  In  this process,  the
approver  satellites perform consensus and add
blocks to the blockchain, while the verifiers verify
the blocks. This network achieves a throughput of
1956 TX/s within 6.87 seconds (  3 → 5  ).
(Phase 3)  Real-Time Decision Making
New debris data is validated and stored in secure
blocks under 6.87 seconds, providing near real-
time updates well  before  the next  120-second
measurement  cycle. The blockchain ledger en-
sures the integrity of  the data, enabling trusted
access to ground stations and inter-satellite com-
munication.
Having outlined these key phases, we now summarize the
main characteristics, as shown in Table III:
Table III: Characteristics of the integrated network.
Network Characteristics
Data Collection  Optical observations every 120 s
Size  24 satellites
Consensus  Decentralized role-based consensus
Network Performance
Transaction Rate  1967 TX/s
Response time  7 seconds for full validation
We also highlight several directions for future work based
on the assumptions we made and observations gathered:
• Assumptions on Trusted Nodes and Links: The GMA
node assumes that trusted nodes and links are available
during the registration process and all throughout oper-
ations. Future research should explore dynamic configu-
rations for this function, as well as strategies to handle
potential faults in all nodes (GMA included).
• Delays and Timely Validation: Delays  in processing
or communication could impact  the performance and
validation speed. Research is needed to develop adaptive

validation mechanisms  that  adjust  to varying network
conditions and ensure timely updates.
• Scalability: As the satellite network grows, scaling the
consensus mechanism to handle more transactions with-
out sacrificing performance is a key challenge as well as
the optical network’s observability. Future studies should
focus on scaling the network to benefit  from reduced
latency as highlighted in [6].

This paper presents a novel blockchain-enabled architecture
for efficient decentralized space surveillance. Our simulation
results indicate that a network under 30 nodes achieves op-
timal  throughput and response time. We also compare our
architecture with a fully participatory consensus model, where
all nodes perform both verification and approval tasks. Across
all scenarios, our approach demonstrates a 9× improvement in
both throughput and response time compared to the full partici-
patory consensus, highlighting the efficiency gains achieved by
assigning dedicated roles for verification and approval. Future
work will explore the impact of faulty nodes and potential
security threats on network performance.
